thread.title: Bill would make Thurgood Marshall’s school a historic site
thread.title_full: Bill would make Thurgood Marshall’s school a historic site

author: Frederick N. Rasmussen
title: Bill would make Thurgood Marshall’s school a historic site
text:
Maryland lawmakers introduced legislation this week to designate Thurgood Marshall’s former Baltimore elementary school as a National Historic Site.
Congressman Kweisi Mfume, joined by Sens. Ben Cardin and Chris Van Hollen, introduced the Justice Thurgood Marshall National Historic Site Establishment Act of 2024 in both the House and Senate.
The bill seeks to designate Baltimore City Public School 103, located on Division Street in West Baltimore’s Upton neighborhood, as a National Historic Site, making it part of the National Park Service.
Justice Thurgood Marshall, the first African American appointed to the U.S. Supreme Court by President Lyndon B. Johnson in 1967, grew up nearby on Division Street. He attended P.S. 103, a segregated African American elementary school, from 1914 to 1921. The son of a Pullman porter and a teacher, Marshall became a pioneering NAACP civil rights lawyer, winning 29 of 32 cases before the Supreme Court, including the landmark Brown v. Board of Education decision in 1954, which declared segregation in public education unconstitutional.
“I, too, spent my teenage years growing up on Division Street. I knew Justice Marshall, I interacted with him, and I have always been inspired by this native son of Baltimore who touched the lives of so many — including my own,” Mfume said in a statement announcing the proposed legislation.
“Supreme Court Justice Thurgood Marshall’s accomplishments, intellect, and determination are inspiring for this generation and all those to come,” Cardin said. “His history — his upbringing and early education, his challenges and successes — are intertwined with Baltimore history and need to be preserved and celebrated.”
For the Rev. Dr. Alvin Hathaway, president and CEO of Beloved Community Services Corp., the legislation represents the realization of a long-held vision. His organization led a $15 million effort, including $1 million in congressionally directed funding, to restore the school, which was built in 1877 for white immigrant children before becoming a segregated school in 1911. Restoration was completed last December, and the building opened to the public this July as the Justice Thurgood Marshall Amenity Center.
“Justice Thurgood Marshall will be to Baltimore as The Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. is to Atlanta,” Hathaway said. “The Thurgood Marshall National Historic Site will be an economic catalyst the Upton/Marble Hill community needs to ignite a renaissance in cultural and historic storytelling.”
“It will be a place that empowers, educates, and informs future generations on the importance of early education as the vehicle that shapes our society. It will also be the place where we continue the outstanding civil rights legacy of this community.”
“This will bring even greater recognition to the place where a young Thurgood Marshall got his start, inviting visitors from across the nation to learn from our past and inspiring them to light the way to a better future for our nation — just as Marshall would have wanted,” Van Hollen said.
Have a news tip? Contact Frederick N. Rasmussen at frasmussen@baltsun.com and 410-332-6536.
